summary: There are n requested ball outcomes and each may depend on a board in every one of m rows, giving a worst-case matrix-transition lower bound matched by direct simulation.

optimality.argument: Families of compatible alternating rows let Theta(n) balls traverse Theta(m) transitions. Changing a board on one such path can turn a successful transition into a wall or V trap and alter its reported output. A correct worst-case algorithm must resolve Theta(mn) path-relevant board relationships. Simulating each ball through each reachable row performs O(1) work per relationship and matches the Omega(mn) bound.

replacement_checks.evidence: Compatible row families keep Theta(n) distinct ball paths alive across Theta(m) rows, and an adversarial board change on a path can alter the corresponding exit.
Each of n balls examines at most m rows and performs constant-time wall and adjacent-direction checks per row.
Authored fixtures cover both walls, both single-column directions, immediate and late V traps, alternating rows, shifted exits, and mixed successful and stuck paths.
